summary: China's AI advantage via distillation of Western frontier models is eroding as labs lock down reasoning traces and stop releasing frontier models openly
summary_extended: Baker argues that China's AI advantage through industrial-scale distillation of Western frontier models is structurally eroding as leading Western labs move to lock down reasoning traces and cease open releases of frontier models. He cites DeepSeek as the canonical example, noting it was built using approximately 160,000 reasoning traces derived from OpenAI's o1/o3 models, and that Chinese actors run roughly 100,000 endpoints systematically distilling across every available API. The core thesis is that this distillation arbitrage only works when frontier models are openly accessible, and that trend is reversing — he points to moves like the locking down of model weights and reasoning traces (referencing what appears to be a frontier model called 'Mythos' or similar) as early signals of this structural shift. The investment implication is that Western frontier AI labs gain a durable competitive moat as the distillation loophole closes, widening the capability gap between US and Chinese AI over the medium term. However, since the primary beneficiaries — OpenAI and Anthropic — are private, there is no directly actionable US-listed equity play implied by this thesis.
detail: Baker notes China became very good at industrial-scale distillation (e.g., DeepSeek built from ~160K reasoning traces from o1/o3). They run 100K endpoints distilling across every available API. But this advantage disappears if frontier models stop being released openly. Mythos (likely Mythic or a frontier model) is a sign of things to come in locking down distillation.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a structural trend with specific data points (160K reasoning traces) but some details are garbled in transcript
quote_fragment: all that goes away if people stop releasing these models at the frontier
